item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While I love this, as a Chicagoan I must ask;

Did you mean Lake Shore Drive instead of Lake Shore Avenue?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with this. Even though the addition of "spicyghost" makes the forum post make sense in context, it doesn't really feel enough like a creepypasta. Urban legend? Sure. But creepypastas feel different than that, kinda more personal. It also doesn't help that, aside from the false elements, it feels like telling me about the anomaly twice. I already know what happened to Rudy, so the post just seems redundant.

I do like the line "Fuck you want me to do about that?", but I agree with A Random Day that the interview feels too monology. Sure, people can talk like that in real life, but it still feels forced in here. The difference between fiction and reality is that fiction has to make sense and all that.

I think that the story would be better if Rudy's backstory was explored through his current Swamp Thing situation. Showing him stabbing himself and shifting between moans of pain and advertising newspapers does a much better job at building the character than just someone telling us what happened.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel kinda sorry for the being though…

The ambivalence of this is worrying.

Yes, you should feel pity for the child who has been tortured for over a century.

item.title: Re: Site Criticism Team - Staff Review
item.content:encoded:
I see where the negative elements of this crit are coming from, and they entirely stem from different headcanons of how big the Spirit was. In my headcanon (which in turn is the canon of Black as Night, the series this is technically part of), the Spirit was large enough that they are "above Veil" and knowledge of the Spirit and Chappell is as common as the Chicago Outfit and Capone. Rumors that they were anomalous — or at the very least, had a fondness for the anomalous — are slightly less common, but are often connected to the group. Only groups that are "behind the Veil" and Parawatch take these rumors seriously — Parawatch are mostly crackpots anyway.

I've added a paragraph to try and make this perspective more clear. I understand the Parawatch section sits in a weird cross of headcanon — right at an intersection of "just public enough" on the half of the Spirit and "just knowledgable enough" on the half of Parawatch — to grok poorly for some users. For the most part, it is "Chicago Spirit's association with the occult leaks, and Parawatch eats it up without a trace of doubt" rather than "Parawatch is actually informed".

item.title: Site Criticism Team - Staff Review
item.content:encoded:
Overall, I found myself enjoying this piece. Your creature design is incredibly gross, and I think is the strongest aspect of the piece. A self-hating entity made of blood and trimmings is supremely gross and holy shit I wish I had come up with it myself. Additionally, adding the irony that Chappell's murder of Rudy turned his existence into sheer torture every time his name was used to incite fear was a really interesting choice that I enjoyed from a literary perspective.

That being said, I do have to agree with ARD that the Parawatch post overstepped a touch. Ultimately, the author knew way too much about things they shouldn't have, and it conflicted with my current understanding of Parawatch as a GoI. I think this is easily remedied by changing just a few details. If they didn't reference the Spirit and Chappell, that'd be an excellent start. Additionally, simplifying the creature design to one that aligns closer to a more cliché horror monster would up the amateur message board feeling of the piece and better justify the Foundation not taking down such a targeted post.

Despite my issues with the Parawatch section, I still enjoyed this enough to upvote.

Disclaimer: Opinions expressed in this review do not reflect the opinions of SCP Wiki staff as a whole. Please respond to TheMightyMcB or contact the captain of the Site Criticism Team, SoullessSingularity, for any questions or concerns relating to this review.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a few different moving parts here, but the only one that really interested me was the exploration log with Rudy the Swamp-Thing. The Parawatch post has too much information on the Chicago Spirit to capture the urban-legend atmosphere that makes Parawatch interesting as a GOI. Richard Chappell's log falls into a weird uncanny valley place between monologue and written diatribe without the strengths of either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good job. A well-written article with an interesting story.
I feel kinda sorry for the being though…

item.title: Remember Rudy Benson
item.content:encoded:
The inspiration for this article, is, in the article. The second image inspired the rest - the story of Rudy Benson (which was told to me by PeppersGhost) stuck with me for a while and bounced in my head. At first, it was the Chicago Spirit's version of a memetic kill-hazard on their 001 proposal (which I abandoned writing). Then, it became a simple warning. Finally, it was this. The second image is the source of all this, and really boils down to the following: What if "Remember, Rudy Benson?" was an imperative and subject, rather than imperative and object?

This SCP has been a long time coming. The first version of the ending was a very dry and clinical Foundation experiment in which they brought Rudy back to sapience, and it was then a series of logs spotting Rudy over the years. Only this past week did I have the idea of bringing Parawatch into this, which practically wrote itself. The Exploration log followed, since Rudy needed to make another appearance in his article.

A massive thanks to PeppersGhost for talking to me about Rudy Benson initially. The information that Chappell gives in his log is inspired by what Peppers told me about Rudy Benson, and that story was originally conceived of by him. Everything after Rudy being exsanguinated (and his subsequent turn to swamp thing) is me going off that. Peppers also looked this over at various points, so many thanks for that crit.

Additional thanks to Rounderhouse (who crit this twice), Gee and rattles, who also looked at this.

Attributions:

  • Bubbly.jpg is from here and was released into the public domain by the US Geological Survey, a US Government Branch.
  • benson.jpg is initially from here and is in the public domain, as it was published before 1924. It was later edited for me by PeppersGhost (who added the typewriter text).
  • In the interest of clarity, the quote from The Jungle by Upton Sinclair is, indeed, from The Jungle by Upton Sinclair, which is in the public domain, as it was published in 1905.
